nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich reef areas at depths of approximately 5–25 m; generally found singly or in pairs

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented grazing primarily on filamentous algae, sponges, tunicates, and small invertebrates such as copepods found among the rocks; trophic level data suggests a herbivore-leaning omnivorous diet. In captivity tends to accept a varied diet including spirulina, marine algae, nori, high-quality angelfish preparations containing sponge material, mysis shrimp, and other meaty frozen items. Frequent small feedings throughout the day are generally advised. Constant grazing behavior is reported; aquarists are commonly advised to avoid scrubbing all algae off rockwork during maintenance so the fish retains its natural food source.

imageCandidates.notes: Bernard Picton; 2048x1536. Tack-sharp full lateral view showing dark velvety reticulated body, bright yellow tail, and silvery-white dorsal/anal outline with blue edge - best diagnostic clarity.
Matthieu Sontag; 1255x941. Crisp aquarium shot, whole fish in frame with vivid yellow tail and white dorsal line clearly visible against live rock.
Rohan Arthur (via iNaturalist/GBIF); wild in-situ dark-background shot showing full coloration and pattern; slight motion softness so kept as non-recommended extra for variety.

summary: The Xanthurus Cream Angelfish (Apolemichthys xanthurus) is a large angelfish from the Western Indian Ocean, recognizable by its dark velvety body, bright yellow tail, and silvery-white dorsal outline. It is generally considered one of the hardier large angelfish but tends to be aggressive and is best suited to large fish-only or FOWLR systems.
careNotes: The Xanthurus Cream Angelfish is generally considered one of the hardier large angelfish once acclimated, and is commonly described as adapting readily to aquarium life. Some retail listings rate the care level as 'easy', but the species' requirement for a large mature aquarium, plentiful live rock for grazing, and its pronounced aggression toward tankmates make it more appropriate for intermediate-level keepers. Temperature shows a notable difference between aquarium recommendations and wild-habitat records: hobby sources recommend 22.2–25.6°C (72–78°F), while wild-habitat data records a mean of approximately 28.2°C (range 25.4–29.3°C). The lower aquarium-oriented range reflects long-term captive husbandry experience, though the wild data suggests this species likely tolerates warmer water without harm. Adding the angel last to an established system, after other tankmates have already claimed territory, is commonly recommended to reduce aggression. The species has been reported to spawn in captivity occasionally but is not commercially bred at scale; tank-bred specimens are available from a growing number of specialist suppliers.
breedingNotes: Apolemichthys xanthurus is reported to be a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite like most angelfishes, with no external sexual dimorphism except a typical size difference between dominant males and females. Broadcast spawning behavior has been observed. Captive breeding has been achieved on a limited scale, with tank-bred juveniles starting to appear in the trade through specialist suppliers, but consistent home-aquarium reproduction remains uncommon.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Apolemichthys xanthurus, Bennett 1833), family (Pomacanthidae), order (Acanthuriformes), max adult size (15.0 cm TL), native range (Western Indian Ocean from Mauritius to India and Sri Lanka), depth range (5–25 m), and wild temperature (mean 28.2°C, range 25.4–29.3°C). Wild temperature noted but aquarium-hobby ranges used for waterParameters.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6–8 in / 15–20 cm), lifespan (up to 10 years), tank minimum (125 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH, salinity, dKH, diet (primarily herbivorous in wild), and aggressive temperament. Notes constant-grazing behavior and broadcast spawning.

fish.breedingNotes: Like most angelfish, the Xanthurus Cream Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite — females can change sex to become dominant males — and is a broadcast spawner that shows no reliable external differences between the sexes other than a typical size advantage in dominant males. Captive breeding remains uncommon in home aquariums, though tank-bred juveniles have begun appearing through specialist suppliers.
